Scorsese's most thrilling affair, if not his purest. There's less detail in the heart, but it's one of the most aesthetic and structurally creative films of his career. So much going on, yet none of it is bogged down by plot. What moments of reprieve there are, usually exist for a heart thumping bit of character development, that hits just the right bit of sincere. Letting you know these are living, breathing characters. Most of which are being pushed to their very limits. The result is some truly remarkable cinema.

Nicholson, Damon, and Dicaprio make for an absolute powerhouse trio in this classic film. I have had this on my watch list for quite some time, and although it wasn't as rock solid as I was expecting, it's ending was right up my alley. Aside from the phenomenal acting and the satisfying conclusion, I was only impressed with the simplicity of which a complex double-rat story was told. I never felt lost trying to keep track of who worked for who and who was spying on who, because the filmmakers laid it out so clearly for me.

Martin Scorsese’s The Departed is a blistering crime thriller that distills the director’s long-standing obsessions—identity, loyalty, and moral rot—into one of his most tightly wound films. Set against the raw, profane rhythms of Boston’s underworld, the movie thrives on paranoia, with tension escalating through razor-sharp editing and a narrative built on constant betrayal. Leonardo DiCaprio brings aching vulnerability to his role as an undercover cop unraveling from the inside, while Matt Damon embodies a chilling, hollow ambition, making their parallel descents feel tragically inevitable. Anchored by Jack Nicholson’s feral, unpredictable performance and driven by Scorsese’s muscular direction, The Departed is both a masterfully engineered genre film and a cynical meditation on the cost of living a lie.

This is another cop movie with a star-studded cast, dieting on a steady stream of curse words and F-bombs. It also contains gratuitous violence, basically terminating all the characters. The crudeness sinks to new lows that I have never seen before, capping it off with a nasty reference to a 13 year old boy. I don't understand why all these stars would agree to this script. And I don't know why it gets so many good ratings. Overall, it's disgusting, a maggot infested rotten tomato.

The Departed is a wild movie with amazing performances and perfect cinematography. The characters are layered and well-written. I obviously gravitated towards the morally positive characters, but my favourite was actually Dignam, who I'd consider morally ambiguous. The dialogues are perfectly-written. I really don't have anything else to say about the writing, because it's just awesome. The direction is great, but I also wasn't expecting any less. I haven't seen many Scorsese films so far, but this is completely different from the ones I have. The lighting is perfect and the camera movements are amazing and very immersive. All in all, the cinematography is flawless. The editing of Thelma Schoonmaker is more than perfect, the Academy Award definitely went to the right place. Some of the visual effects looked off to me. I couldn't decide whether a few things were matte paintings or just bad CGI, but either way it bugged me a little bit. The song choices were hit or miss. There were ones that I loved, but some others just didn't fit in my opinion. The acting performances are obviously amazing, but Mark Wahlberg is just unexpectedly out of this world in the film. To be honest, this is the first performance of his I really enjoyed, but then I haven't seen Boogie Nights for example. The Departed is amazing, but I had a few issues with it that made me rate it lower than 10/10.
